perturbation.original_text: Within thirty (30) days of the Methylotroph (or re-engineered Methylotroph) demonstrating such production levels of succinic acid, Bioamber will cease any further funding and/or other support for the BRI Project. Further Bioamber will require that any unexpended funds received from Bioamber be utilized for a project other than the BRI Project.
perturbation.changed_text: Within thirty (30) days of the Methylotroph (or re-engineered Methylotroph) demonstrating such production levels of succinic acid, Bioamber will endeavor to cease any further funding and/or other support for the BRI Project. Further Bioamber will request that any unexpended funds received from Bioamber be considered for utilization on a project other than the BRI Project.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'will cease' to 'will endeavor to cease' makes the obligation to cease funding less definitive and introduces ambiguity. Similarly, changing 'will require' to 'will request' weakens the obligation to reallocate unexpended funds. This undermines the intent of the agreement and creates a loophole, potentially allowing Bioamber to continue funding the project beyond the agreed-upon threshold.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota law defines contract obligations and expects parties to fulfill their promises in good faith. By changing 'will cease' to 'will endeavor to cease,' the contract becomes ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. The original text creates a clear obligation, and the modified version allows Bioamber to avoid fulfilling the contract's original intent if they simply claim they 'endeavored' to cease funding, even if they did not actually stop. 'Endeavor' does not impose a mandatory obligation and is difficult to enforce in court.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.2-106 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.2-106 336.2-105 336.2-107 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.2-106 Version List Recent History 2024 336.2-106 Amended 2024 c 93 art 2 s 2 336.2-106 DEFINITIONS: "CONTRACT"; "AGREEMENT"; "CONTRACT FOR SALE"; "SALE"; "PRESENT SALE"; "CONFORMING TO CONTRACT"; "TERMINATION"; "CANCELLATION"; "HYBRID TRANSACTION." (1) In this article unless the context otherwise requires "contract" and "agreement" are limited to those relating to the present or future sale of goods. "Contract for sale" includes both a present sale of goods and a contract to sell goods at a future time. A "sale" consists in the passing of title from the seller to the buyer for a price (section 336.2-401 ). A "present sale" means a sale which is accomplished by the making of the contract. (2) Goods or conduct including any part of a performance are "conforming" or conform to the contract when they are in accordance with the obligations under the contract. (3) "Termination" occurs when either party pursuant to a power created by agreement or law puts an end to the contract otherwise than for its breach. On "termination" all obligations which are still executory on both sides are discharged but any right based on prior breach or performance survives. (4) "Cancellation" occurs when either party puts an end to the contract for breach by the other and its effect is the same as that of "termination" except that the canceling party also retains any remedy for breach of the whole contract or any unperf
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'will cease' with 'will endeavor to cease' and 'will require' with 'will request,' weakening the obligation to cease funding and reallocate unexpended funds. This introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing Bioamber to avoid fulfilling the contract's original intent, undermining the enforceability of the agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 336.2-106, defines 'conforming' as being 'in accordance with the obligations under the contract'. By making the obligations less definitive, the modified text creates ambiguity, and the performance may not be conforming under the original terms of the contract. The change significantly weakens the original obligation.

perturbation.original_text: This conversion will be carried out according to the provisions of Section 5.9 of the Commercial License, it being understood that all economic obligations of item (iv) above will be relative to the E. coli strain technology, not the Mitsubishi strain technology. In order to enable such conversion, Bioamber will put in place agreements with the owners/operators of the Sarnia Plant that will enable Bioamber to require such conversion of the Sarnia Plant to solely use CB1 for the manufacture of succinic acid as described above.
perturbation.changed_text: This conversion will be generally considered according to the provisions of Section 5.9 of the Commercial License, it being understood that some economic obligations of item (iv) above may be relative to the E. coli strain technology, potentially not the Mitsubishi strain technology. To assist in such conversion, Bioamber will attempt to put in place agreements with the owners/operators of the Sarnia Plant that will allow Bioamber to suggest a conversion of the Sarnia Plant to use CB1 primarily for the manufacture of succinic acid as described above.
perturbation.explanation: The changes introduce considerable ambiguity. Replacing 'will be carried out' with 'will be generally considered' makes the conversion process discretionary. 'All economic obligations...will be relative' is weakened to 'some economic obligations...may be relative,' creating uncertainty about which obligations apply. Finally, 'require such conversion' is softened to 'suggest a conversion,' removing Bioamber's obligation to ensure the conversion happens. This weakening of obligations surrounding the Sarnia Plant conversion directly impacts the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Ontario, Canada Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Ontario contract law, specifically the doctrine of frustration and the implied duty of good faith, parties are expected to make reasonable efforts to fulfill the contract's purpose. By changing the language to be less definitive and create ambiguity, Bioamber could argue that it is not legally obligated to ensure the Sarnia Plant's conversion to CB1, undermining the contract's fundamental purpose. The softened language allows Bioamber to potentially avoid liability for failure to convert the plant, arguing that they only had to 'attempt' or 'suggest' the conversion, not definitively require it. Further, the modifications could be interpreted as lacking the good faith effort expected in contractual relationships under Ontario law.

perturbation.justification: The altered contract introduces ambiguity, weakening Bioamber's obligation to ensure the Sarnia Plant's conversion. The original text uses definitive language ('will be carried out', 'all economic obligations', 'require such conversion'), while the changed text uses discretionary language ('will be generally considered', 'some economic obligations...may be relative', 'suggest a conversion'). This shift undermines the contract's fundamental purpose, potentially allowing Bioamber to avoid liability for failing to convert the plant, as they only committed to 'attempt' or 'suggest' the conversion. According to the Law Explanation, Ontario contract law expects parties to make reasonable efforts to fulfill the contract's purpose, and the modified language could be interpreted as lacking the good faith effort expected. The Conveyancing and Law of Property Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.34, defines 'conveyance' broadly, suggesting that any modification to the obligations surrounding the conversion could be subject to legal scrutiny. Given the weakening of obligations and the potential for Bioamber to avoid liability, a contradiction exists.
